name: Serqet
description: Serqet, also known as Selket, Selqet, or Serkis, is a powerful goddess in Egyptian mythology, primarily associated with protection from venomous creatures, particularly scorpions. Her name means 'she who tightens the throat,' referring both to the constriction of a scorpion's sting and to Serqet's power to heal and protect against it. She is depicted as a woman with a scorpion on her head, or sometimes as a scorpion with a woman's head. Serqet was believed to be a fierce protector of the pharaoh and was often depicted alongside other protective deities such as Isis, Nephthys, and Neith. She played a vital role in funerary rituals, guarding the canopic jar containing the intestines, ensuring the deceased's safe passage into the afterlife. Serqet was also a goddess of healing and magic, invoked to cure scorpion stings and other ailments. Her worship was widespread throughout Egypt, and she was revered for her ability to both inflict and prevent harm. She was considered a skilled magician and a powerful protector, making her an essential figure in Egyptian religious beliefs and practices. Serqet's association with scorpions connects her to both danger and healing, highlighting the duality of her nature and her importance in maintaining balance in the world.
subtitle: Goddess of Protection and Healing

keyMyths.title: Serqet and Horus
keyMyths.description: Serqet protected the infant Horus from scorpion stings while he was hidden from Set. Her magic and protective abilities were crucial in ensuring Horus' survival.

keyMyths.title: The Four Protective Goddesses
keyMyths.description: Serqet, along with Isis, Nephthys, and Neith, guarded the deceased pharaoh in the afterlife. Each goddess protected a specific canopic jar, ensuring the preservation of the organs.

keyMyths.title: Serqet's Healing Power
keyMyths.description: Serqet was invoked to heal scorpion stings and other venomous bites. Her priests and priestesses were skilled in using spells and remedies to counteract the effects of poison.

sources.author: Richard H. Wilkinson
sources.work: The Complete Gods and Goddesses of Ancient Egypt

sources.author: George Hart
sources.work: A Dictionary of Egyptian Gods and Goddesses
